sets.passage: I used to be the kind of person who hated any form of exercise. So when my doctor suggested I start running, I almost (1)___. But my health was poor, and I knew I had to make a (2)___. Everything in me wanted to go back to bed, yet I forced myself to keep (5)___, just to the next tree, then the next. The following days were not much (6)___. My legs ached, the mornings were dark and cold, and progress felt painfully (7)___. Many times I asked myself why I even (8)___. But I made one simple rule: no matter how slow, I would not (9)___ a single morning. Slowly, almost without noticing, things began to (10)___. One day I ran for ten minutes without stopping; another day, the hill that had once (11)___ me felt easy. My body grew stronger, but the real change was in my (12)___: I had proved I could do something hard. Two years later, I (13)___ my first half-marathon. Crossing that finish line, exhausted and overjoyed, I thought back to that first miserable morning. The lesson was (14)___: big achievements are simply small efforts, repeated. sets.passage: In the corner of my grandfather's garage stood an old bicycle, rusty and (1)___, that no one had ridden for thirty years. The bicycle, it turned out, had been his very (5)___, bought with his first wages, and he had ridden it to meet my grandmother on their first (6)___. As we rebuilt it, he seemed to grow younger, his eyes (7)___ with memories. When at last it was finished, gleaming and (8)___ again, my grandfather climbed on and rode slowly around the yard, laughing like a boy. It was one of the happiest moments I had ever (9)___ him. He passed away the following winter. The bicycle now stands in my own home, and I keep it perfectly (10)___. To others it may look like just an old machine, but to me it holds something (11)___. Whenever I polish it, I remember not only my grandfather but the (12)___ he gave me that summer: that the things we repair with our own hands, and the time we (13)___ with the people we love, are never truly (14)___. sets.passage: On my way home from school, I found a (1)___ lying on the pavement. Inside was a thick bundle of cash, more money than I had ever (2)___ at once, along with some cards and a photo of a smiling family. For a moment, I will be honest, I was (3)___: that money could buy so many things I had always wanted. No one had seen me pick it up; no one would ever (4)___. But as I looked at the photo, I imagined how the owner must be (5)___ right now, perhaps having lost money they badly needed. I knew at once what I had to (6)___. Using a card inside, I managed to find a phone (7)___ and called. An hour later, an anxious middle-aged man hurried to meet me. When I handed him the wallet, untouched, his face flooded with (8)___. He explained that the money was for his mother's (9)___ operation, and that losing it had felt like the end of the world. He tried to give me a large (10)___ as thanks, but I (11)___; helping him was reward enough. Walking home with empty pockets but a full (12)___, I felt richer than any amount of money could have made me. I had been (13)___ to keep it, and no one would have known. sets.passage: One windy autumn morning, I found a small bird lying on our doorstep, one wing bent at a strange (1)___. It must have flown into a window. I looked up how to care for an injured bird and followed every instruction (5)___, feeding it tiny amounts of food and keeping it away from noise. The morning I knew it was ready, I carried the box into the garden and opened the (9)___. For a moment the bird hesitated. Then, with a sudden burst of energy, it (10)___ into the sky and was gone. sets.passage: I discovered the little second-hand bookshop by (1)___, ducking in one rainy afternoon merely to escape the downpour. I had never been much of a (2)___; books, to me, were just things we were forced to study at school. The shop was small and crowded, its shelves (3)___ from floor to ceiling with old volumes, and it smelled of dust and paper. To pass the time, I picked up a worn novel at (4)___ and began to read. To my surprise, I could not put it (5)___. The story pulled me in completely, and when I finally looked up, an hour had (6)___ without my noticing.
